sourceMovie.title: Candyman
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Candyman (1992) as a drama theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The drama and horror blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Rosemary's Baby
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Candyman clicked for you, Rosemary's Baby (1968) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and horror, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Candyman?
faq.answer: Try Rosemary's Baby, Repulsion, The Silence of the Lambs—each shares DNA with Candyman while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Candyman?
faq.answer: Candyman (1992) is best described as Drama, Horror, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Candyman part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Candyman Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
